sanskrit_devanagari:
न बुद्धिभेदं जनयेदज्ञानां कर्मसङ्गिनाम् ।
जोषयेत्सर्वकर्माणि विद्वान्युक्तः समाचरन् ॥ २६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
na buddhi-bhedaṁ janayed
ajñānāṁ karma-saṅginām |
joṣayet sarva-karmāṇi
vidvān yuktaḥ samācaran || 26 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)
exact (text body identical with vedabase; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.026 = MBh_06,025.026). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

anvaya.meaning: who are attached to fruitive work

translations.verbatim_quote: So as not to disrupt the minds of ignorant men attached to the fruitive results of prescribed duties, a learned person should not induce them to stop work. Rather, by working in the spirit of devotion, he should engage them in all sorts of activities [for the gradual development of Kṛṣṇa consciousness].
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Vedaiś ca sarvair aham eva vedyaḥ. That is the end of all Vedic rituals. All rituals, all performances of sacrifices, and everything that is put into the Vedas, including all direction for material activities, are meant for understanding Kṛṣṇa, who is the ultimate goal of life.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.26 Vidvan the enlightened man; na janayet, should not create; buddhi-bhedam, disturbance in the beliefs-disturbance in the firm belief, ‘This has to be done; and the result of this action is to be reaped by me’; ajnanam, of the ignorant, of the non-discriminating one; karma-sang
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: na buddhi-bhedaṁ janayet — the wise should not produce a splitting of the intellect — ajñānāṁ karma-saṅginām — in the unwise who are attached to action. Rather joṣayet sarva-karmāṇi — let him cause them to enjoy all actions — vidvān yuktaḥ samācaran — being himself wise, yoked, performing well. The verse therefore makes the public-facing rule explicit: the wise actor should not signal that action is beneath him, because that signal will be read by the unwise as 'action is to be abandoned,' and they will abandon their own karma without having reached the realisation that would warrant it.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.26 Do not bewilder the minds of ignorant aspirants by saying that there is, besides Karma Yoga, another way to the vision of the self. They cannot practise Jnana Yoga on account of their incomplete knowledge of the self, and attachment to action.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the wise actor must not produce buddhi-bheda — confusion of intellect — in the unwise who are still attached to action. The mechanism: if the wise actor visibly disengages or speaks of action as beneath him, the unwise will be drawn to imitate (per 3.21) without having reached the inner state that allows safe disengagement. The remedy: vidvān yuktaḥ samācaran — the wise actor, yoked, performing well — should joṣayet sarva-karmāṇi — cause the unwise to take pleasure in all actions, including their own.

positions.rendering: let not the wise disrupt the minds of the ignorant who are attached to fruitive action
the wise should not create discord in the intellects of the ignorant who are attached to fruitive actions

buddhi-bheda = creating doubt in the intellect; the wise must not produce in the unwise the doubt that would lead them to abandon their own prescribed karma without having reached the realisation that exempts from it

the wise should not generate confusion in the unwise by preaching detachment they cannot yet implement; rather, by acting himself yuktaḥ — yoked — he should encourage the unwise to enjoy their own actions

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both commentators read buddhi-bheda as a specific failure mode: the wise actor publicly framing his action as transcendent or beneath him, with the result that the watching unwise lose confidence in their own karma without gaining the realisation that would exempt them. The translators capture the surface but the technical claim is more specific — the wise must visibly act with full engagement to keep the unwise from drift.

literal_meaning: Let the wise one not unsettle the buddhi (intellect) of the unwise who are attached to action; rather, acting himself in a yoked manner, let him cause others to enjoy all action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The exemplar argument's implementation rule. The wise should not preach disengagement to the unwise (who would mishear it as license); rather, by acting yoked (yuktaḥ) and well (samācaran), the wise should cause others to remain happily engaged in their own karma. Krishna explicitly names buddhi-bheda — disturbing the intellect — as a wrong move by the wise.
